summary: Big news from the Southeast Beltline corridor today as Reynoldstown finally sees movement on that long-stalled townhome project near the trail, plus Decatur's getting some much-needed 'missing middle' housing. Meanwhile, the city's dealing with ICE agents at the airport and firefighters are still waiting on their union contract.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Stalled Beltline Project Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After nearly five years of sitting empty, that cleared corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. The 85-unit townhome and condo project is moving forward under new ownership, bringing much-needed housing to the hot Reynoldstown market. It's been a long wait for neighbors who've been staring at vacant dirt since the site was cleared.
topStory.body:
You know that empty lot on Chester Avenue in Reynoldstown that's been taunting neighbors for years? Well, grab your hard hats because construction is finally happening. After the site sat dormant since being cleared in 2021, Atlanta-based Embry Development has taken over the 85-unit project from national developer Toll Brothers and is making real progress.

The development, now called Camber Crossing, sits just a block from the Eastside Trail and about a block west of where all the Beltline action happens. We're talking townhomes and for-sale condos on 2 acres that should help address the housing crunch in one of the city's most desirable neighborhoods. The fact that it's walkable to the Beltline, close to Memorial Drive's restaurant scene, and still (relatively) affordable makes this a big deal.

Embry Development's managing partner says they're committed to actually finishing what Toll Brothers started, which is music to the ears of anyone who's had to walk past that construction fence for the past few years. Given how hot the Reynoldstown market has gotten, these units should move fast once they're ready.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction activity at the Camber Crossing development site on Chester Avenue in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur Gets Affordable 'Missing Middle' Housing Village
stories.summary: The Cottages at Midway is bringing 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards to unincorporated Decatur, targeting buyers priced out of the main market where median home values hit $700,000. It's exactly the kind of 'missing middle' housing Atlanta desperately needs. The soft launch starts next week for this village-style community.
stories.body:
Finally, someone's building housing that normal people can actually afford near Decatur. The Cottages at Midway is taking two underused lots on Midway Road and turning them into 18 standalone houses arranged around shared courtyards and green space. The goal is to create something between a single-family house and an apartment, hence the 'missing middle' label.

With median home values in Decatur proper hitting over $700,000, this mixed-income community is trying to serve people who make decent money but aren't tech executives. The village-style setup means you get your own house but share some outdoor space with neighbors, which honestly sounds pretty nice. Plus you're still close enough to walk to downtown Decatur and hop on MARTA.

The developers are doing a soft launch next week to start gauging interest, which is smart given how quickly things move in this market. The fact that it's on a major commercial corridor but still feels residential should appeal to people who want urban conveniences without the urban chaos.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for the Cottages at Midway development showing village-style housing layout

stories.headline: West End's Beltline Townhomes Starting to Take Shape
stories.summary: The GW Parkside project along the Southwest trail corridor is finally rising from the ground after a year of demolition and prep work. Ackerman & Co. is bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to the area, with the first seven units hitting the market this spring. It's rare to see new for-sale housing along this stretch of the Beltline.
stories.body:
Remember when Ackerman & Co. started tearing stuff down along Hopkins Street in the West End last year? Well, now we're seeing why. The GW Parkside project is finally taking vertical shape, bringing 23 new townhomes to a stretch of the Beltline that doesn't see a lot of new for-sale housing.

The first seven units are supposed to hit the market this spring, which should give us a good sense of appetite for new construction in this part of town. The developer already owns Lee + White, so they know the area and understand what works along the Southwest Connector trail. Having walkable access to that whole complex of offices, restaurants, and events is a pretty compelling selling point.

What's interesting is that for-sale townhomes are relatively rare along this stretch of the Beltline compared to apartments and condos. The West End has been slower to gentrify than places like the Old Fourth Ward, so these units could be a bellwether for how fast things are changing in the neighborhood.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes under construction in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: This new spot on Elizabeth Street just off the Freedom Park Trail opened today with soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis. Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez went from pop-up to permanent, and their Asian-American comfort food menu looks promising for lunch and dinner.
stories.body:
There's a new player in Inman Park's food scene, and it's exactly the kind of casual-but-serious spot the neighborhood needs. Mushi Ni opened today on Elizabeth Street (you know, right off the Freedom Park Trail where you can grab food after a walk), and chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez are bringing their pop-up success to a permanent home.

The menu hits that sweet spot of Asian-American comfort food that's both familiar and interesting. We're talking soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, and crab Rangoon puffs for snacking, plus heartier Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25. The cocktail program includes Thai tea martinis, which honestly sounds perfect for this neighborhood.

Le and Jimenez aren't newbies to the scene (she worked for Gordon Ramsay in NYC before moving to Atlanta), and they've spent time figuring out what works through their pop-up phase. The location is smart too, catching both foot traffic from Freedom Park and people walking between Inman Park and Little Five Points.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's Eastside Food Hall Opens in April
stories.summary: The guy who created NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta food concept to the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works will feature six chef-driven concepts with craft cocktails and outdoor dining when it opens mid-April.
stories.body:
If you've been wondering what's happening at that big development near East Lake Golf Club, here's your answer: Robert Montwaid is opening another food hall. The man behind NYC's famous Gansevoort Market (and two other Atlanta spots) is launching Vela Park Food Works as the centerpiece of the nearly 12-acre rental community.

This isn't just another food court situation. We're talking six different chef-driven concepts under one roof, plus craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space. Montwaid knows how to curate food vendors, and his track record suggests this won't be the usual mix of chains and mediocre options you see in most food halls.

The timing works out well, with a mid-April opening that should catch the spring weather when people actually want to sit outside. The East Lake location is interesting too, serving both the new residents of Vela Park and the broader eastside community that's been looking for more dining options in that area.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works food hall

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin Heat to the Beltline
stories.summary: The new smokehouse at Ansley Mall has six 22-foot smokers running 24 hours a day in a glass-walled loft overlooking the pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. Pitmaster John Lewis is serving some of the best brisket in Atlanta, with that Texas-style heat and technique you can't find elsewhere in the city.
stories.body:
There's a new barbecue game in town, and it's unlike anything Atlanta has seen before. Lewis Barbecue just opened at Ansley Mall with a setup that's part restaurant, part theater. The smokehouse is upstairs in a spacious loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline, and you can watch six massive 22-foot smokers running around the clock.

Pitmaster John Lewis helped weld those smokers out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and they're turning out brisket that rivals anything you'll find in Texas. The operation runs on an industrial schedule with smokers staggered in eight-hour shifts, which means consistent quality and the kind of volume that can actually serve Atlanta's appetite for good barbecue.

The Ansley Mall location is perfect for this concept, catching both Midtown office workers and Beltline foot traffic. Plus, having those dramatic windows where you can actually see the smoking operation adds a level of transparency and showmanship that makes the whole experience more engaging.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Street Nearly Finished
stories.summary: After almost a decade in the works, Decatur's big push to calm traffic on West Howard Avenue is wrapping up. The mile-long project adds protected bike lanes, new sidewalks, and traffic calming between downtown and East Lake MARTA. It's exactly the kind of infrastructure that makes walking and biking actually safe.
stories.body:
Decatur has been talking about making West Howard Avenue less of a car raceway for years, and they're finally almost done. The Complete Streets project stretches about a mile from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School all the way to the city limits at East Lake MARTA station, and it's a dramatic transformation from the high-speed drag strip it used to be.

The big addition is a two-way protected cycle track with concrete barriers for most of the route, which means you can actually bike between downtown Decatur and the eastside without playing chicken with cars. They've also added new sidewalks and infrastructure that should make the whole corridor feel more like a neighborhood street than a highway.

This kind of project is exactly what other ITP cities should be copying. West Howard was always a dangerous mess for anyone not in a car, and now it's going to be a real transportation option that connects two major transit hubs. The timing is perfect too, with all the development happening around East Lake MARTA.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lanes on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deployed to Help with Airport Security
stories.summary: With TSA agents calling out sick at record rates during the government shutdown, Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents are now working at Hartsfield-Jackson to help with security (but not immigration enforcement). The airport is still recommending four-hour arrival times as the federal funding crisis drags on.
stories.body:
The partial government shutdown is creating some weird scenes at the world's busiest airport. ICE agents in tactical gear are now patrolling Hartsfield-Jackson to help with security operations as TSA agents continue working without pay and calling out sick at unprecedented rates. Mayor Dickens was quick to clarify that these agents won't be doing immigration enforcement during this deployment.

The situation is still pretty chaotic for travelers, with the airport recommending people arrive four hours early for domestic flights. TSA agents are understandably demoralized about working without paychecks, and nearly 3,500 called out Monday alone. That's creating unpredictable security lines that can stretch for hours during peak times.

What's frustrating is how this federal dysfunction is hitting Atlanta particularly hard because we're the world's busiest airport. Travelers are getting whipsawed by uncertainty, never knowing if they'll breeze through security in 30 minutes or wait for four hours. It's a mess that's entirely preventable if Congress would just do its job and fund the government.
stories.imageAlt: TSA security checkpoint at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Atlanta Council Advances ICE Controls, Fire Union Contract Dispute
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions to block ICE detention facilities in the city and require documentation when federal agents are present during police operations. Meanwhile, dozens of residents criticized Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
Atlanta's City Council is pushing back against federal immigration enforcement while dealing with labor drama at home. The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced two resolutions that would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and require Atlanta police to document any interactions with federal immigration agents during their operations. The full council votes April 20.

But the bigger fireworks came from dozens of residents who showed up to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever firefighter union contract. Council ratified the deal back in April 2025, but Dickens has been sitting on it ever since, leaving the firefighters' union in limbo and creating a weird standoff between the mayor and council.

The ICE resolutions are mostly symbolic but send a clear message about Atlanta's values, especially with federal agents now deployed at the airport. The firefighter contract issue is more immediately practical since it affects the people who literally run into burning buildings to save lives. Both issues highlight the tension between local control and federal pressure that's becoming a defining theme of Atlanta politics.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting

stories.headline: Atlantic Station and Midtown Crack National Top 10
stories.summary: A new ranking from Niche.com put Atlantic Station at #1 and Midtown at #6 on the 'Best Places to Live in America' list. The rankings considered schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime, and amenities across 18,000 neighborhoods nationwide. Not bad for a city that gets no respect from coastal elites.
stories.body:
Take that, Brooklyn. Atlantic Station just landed the #1 spot on Niche.com's 'Best Places to Live in America' list, with Midtown coming in at #6. That means Atlanta claimed 20% of the national top 10, which is pretty incredible when you think about all the neighborhoods across the country competing for those spots.

Niche looked at public schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime rates, and local amenities across more than 18,000 neighborhoods nationwide. Atlantic Station's combination of walkability, entertainment options, and relative affordability (compared to similar urban districts in other major cities) apparently impressed the algorithm. Midtown's mix of culture, nightlife, and central location sealed its top-10 finish.

What's interesting is that both neighborhoods represent different aspects of Atlanta's appeal. Atlantic Station is the newer, master-planned urban experience, while Midtown has that established cultural scene with the High Museum, Fox Theatre, and Piedmont Park. Together, they show that Atlanta offers multiple ways to live the urban lifestyle without paying New York or San Francisco prices.
stories.imageAlt: Street view of Atlantic Station development
